text: The development of coordination complexes based on abundant, and therefore cheap, metals with attractive functionalities, such as luminescence, solar energy conversion and catalysis, has seen an overwhelming use of second and third-row transition metals with a d 6 configuration like Ru(II), Os(II) and Ir(III). To date, no d 6 complexes based on first-row transition metals affording photophysical and photochemical properties that can compete with the established ones have been reported. A multitude of studies centered on Fe(II) have failed so far to produce emissive d 6 systems with satisfactory photophysical parameters. Here we report that the isoelectronic Cr(0) can give rise to higher photoluminescence quantum yields (up to 15%) and excited state lifetimes compared to any first-row d 6 metal complexes reported to date, while matching that of a well-studied Os(II) polypyridine complex. By virtue of the emitting excited states, Cr(0) complexes show new avenues of applications in photoredox catalysis for which the metal-to-ligand charge transfer states of first-row d 6 complexes are proactively utilized for the first time. Benchmark chemical reductions occur efficiently under low-energy red illumination. Overall our work demonstrates that photophysics and photochemistry of abundant first-row d 6 metals can be opened using suitable design criteria.
